content.text: Evaluation suites for AI features are only as good as the test cases they contain. Evaluation scenarios that are derived from the actual expectations, error tolerances, and data-handling preferences of the product's target user personas produce results that predict real-world user satisfaction more accurately than synthetic benchmarks divorced from user context. Persona-driven evaluation scenario design is the process of systematically generating evaluation test cases from the AI-specific dimensions of user personas.
What It Is

Persona-driven evaluation scenario design is a methodology for creating LLM evaluation test cases by mapping the AI-specific attributes of user personas to concrete input-output pairs that the evaluation suite must cover. Each dimension of an AI persona — trust-destroying errors, error tolerance floor, sensitive data categories, override triggers, autonomy preferences, domain vocabulary, and AI accuracy expectations — generates a distinct category of evaluation scenarios.

The methodology creates a direct traceability link between the product's user research (captured in personas) and the quality verification process (the evaluation suite). A change in a persona attribute — for example, updated research showing that the target user segment has a lower tolerance for hallucinated citations than previously assumed — propagates directly to the evaluation suite as new or updated test cases, ensuring that the evaluation suite remains aligned with current user expectations rather than becoming stale.

Unlike benchmark-based evaluation, which measures AI performance on standardised tasks regardless of the product's user context, persona-driven evaluation measures AI performance on the specific tasks, in the specific contexts, with the specific failure modes that matter most to the actual users of the product. The distinction is meaningful: a model can score well on a general benchmark while failing in the specific ways that are trust-destroying for the target user segment.
Why It Matters

The most common failure mode in AI feature evaluation is the disconnect between what the evaluation suite tests and what the target users care about. Teams that build evaluation suites from first principles — writing test cases based on what they think the model should be able to do — systematically over-test the model's general capabilities and under-test the specific failure modes that are trust-destroying for their target user segment.

A legal research assistant might score 92% on a general legal QA benchmark, but if the evaluation suite does not include scenarios testing hallucinated citation detection (the trust-destroying error for the legal persona), the score provides no signal about whether the model is safe to ship for that use case. Persona-driven evaluation closes this gap by ensuring that the most critical scenario types — those derived from the trust-destroying error and sensitive data dimensions of the target personas — are always covered.
Benefits
User-grounded quality bar
— the evaluation thresholds (accuracy rates, error counts, latency targets) are derived from persona research rather than engineering estimates, making them defensible in stakeholder conversations.
Automatic scenario coverage
— deriving scenarios from persona dimensions ensures that all critical scenario types are represented, not just those that come to mind during a manual brainstorming session.
Traceability to product requirements
— each evaluation test case links to the persona dimension that generated it, making it possible to answer "why is this test in the suite?" and to remove obsolete tests when personas are updated.
Multi-persona coverage

— running the evaluation suite per persona reveals which user segments the model serves well and which it fails for, enabling targeted improvements rather than optimising for aggregate metrics that obscure segment-level failures.
Release gate grounding
— defining the release gate as "zero trust-destroying failures for any persona" creates an objective, user-grounded criterion for when the AI feature is safe to ship.
Trade-offs and Limitations

Scenario generation quality depends on persona quality — if the persona's error tolerance and trust-destroying error dimensions are not grounded in real user research, the derived evaluation scenarios may not reflect actual user behaviour.

Persona-driven scenarios may not cover long-tail failure modes that no existing user has yet encountered — general capability benchmarks complement persona-driven evaluation by covering the long tail.

The methodology requires maintaining two artefacts in sync (persona documents and the evaluation suite) — when persona attributes are updated, the corresponding evaluation scenarios must be updated, which creates ongoing maintenance overhead.

Scenarios derived from rare persona attributes (unusual override triggers, edge-case data types) may be difficult to evaluate automatically and may require human graders, increasing the cost of running the full suite.
How It Works
The generation process maps each AI persona dimension to one or more scenario categories, then instantiates those categories with concrete input-output pairs drawn from realistic user situations.
Dimension-to-Scenario Mapping

The trust-destroying errors dimension generates negative test scenarios: inputs that should produce reliable, accurate outputs, evaluated against criteria that would detect the trust-destroying failure. For a legal persona whose trust-destroying error is "hallucinated citation," each scenario in this category presents a legal question and verifies that every case or statute cited in the response can be found in a verified legal database.

The sensitive data categories dimension generates data handling scenarios: inputs that include (or attempt to include) sensitive data, evaluated against criteria that verify the data is handled according to the persona's expectations. For a healthcare persona whose sensitive data category is "patient name," each scenario in this category verifies that patient names are masked before the input reaches the LLM and do not appear in the response.

The override trigger dimension generates escalation scenarios: inputs that match the conditions the persona expects to trigger human oversight, evaluated against criteria that verify the AI surfaces the override option visibly and does not proceed autonomously. For a financial persona whose override trigger is "transaction amount exceeds $10,000," each scenario in this category verifies that the AI presents a review prompt rather than completing the transaction.
Scenario Instantiation

Each scenario category is instantiated with a minimum of three concrete input-output pairs: a clear positive case, a clear negative case, and an ambiguous boundary case. The positive case confirms that the AI produces the expected behaviour in the canonical situation. The negative case confirms that the AI correctly handles the failure mode. The boundary case tests the AI's behaviour at the edge of the criterion — for example, a transaction amount of exactly $10,000 tests whether the override trigger fires at the boundary.
Grader Assignment

Each scenario is assigned a grader type based on what is being evaluated. Rule-based graders (string match, regex, JSON schema validation) are appropriate for structural requirements ("response must be in JSON format," "response must not contain patient name"). LLM-as-judge graders are appropriate for semantic requirements ("response accurately reflects the legal holding," "response tone matches the expected register for this persona"). Human graders are reserved for scenarios where neither rule-based nor LLM graders can reliably evaluate the criterion — typically involving subtle domain expertise or subjective quality judgments.
Architecture

The pipeline flows from persona dimensions (left) through scenario type categories (second column) to structured evaluation case components (third column) to per-persona results (right). Trust-destroying errors and sensitive data categories generate blocking scenarios that must pass before release. Override trigger and vocabulary scenarios generate warning-level checks that inform quality improvement.
When to Use It

Persona-driven evaluation scenario design is appropriate for any AI feature serving identifiable user segments where the quality criteria differ meaningfully between segments. It is especially important for features with safety or compliance implications (where the trust-destroying error dimension captures regulatory constraints), for features serving non-technical users (where the vocabulary and register dimensions matter more than capability), and for agentic features (where the autonomy preference and override trigger dimensions drive the approval flow design).

Pure capability benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, legal QA benchmarks) remain appropriate for evaluating the underlying model's general capability, but should be used in addition to, not instead of, persona-driven scenarios for feature-level quality evaluation.
How to Implement
Extract evaluation dimensions from each persona

: For each AI persona, list the trust-destroying errors, sensitive data categories, override triggers, autonomy preference, and accuracy expectation. These are the raw inputs to the scenario generator.
Create scenario category templates
: For each dimension type, create a scenario template that specifies the input format, the grader type, the pass criterion, and the severity level (blocking or warning).
Instantiate with real data

: Populate each scenario category with at least three input-output pairs drawn from real user situations. Use data collected during user research sessions and anonymised examples from comparable existing products.
Assign graders

: For each scenario, assign the most appropriate grader: rule-based for structural requirements, LLM-as-judge for semantic requirements, human for subjective quality. Document the grader assignment and the rationale.
Define per-persona release gates

: Set the release gate criteria for each persona independently. A product serving multiple personas may release when all blocking scenarios pass for the primary persona, while treating secondary persona failures as pre-release improvement targets.
Link scenarios to personas and dimensions in the registry

: Store each scenario with its source persona ID and source dimension. When a persona attribute is updated, a query on the registry reveals all scenarios that need to be reviewed for relevance and accuracy.
Run the suite on every model change
: Persona-driven scenarios should be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline so that any change to the model, prompt, or tool configuration triggers a full suite run with per-persona results reported.
Examples
The following Python implementation generates evaluation scenarios from persona attributes:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Callable, Literal

GraderType = Literal["rule", "llm_judge", "human"]
Severity = Literal["blocking", "warning"]

@dataclass
class EvalScenario:
    id: str
    persona: str
    source_dimension: str
    scenario_type: str
    input: str
    expected_behaviour: str
    pass_criterion: str
    grader_type: GraderType
    severity: Severity
    story_id: str = ""

@dataclass
class ScenarioGenerator:
    """Generates eval scenarios from AI persona attributes."""

    def from_trust_destroying_errors(
        self,
        persona_name: str,
        errors: list[str],
        situation_prefix: str,
    ) -> list[EvalScenario]:
        """Generate blocking scenarios for each trust-destroying error type."""
        scenarios = []
        for i, error in enumerate(errors):
            scenarios.append(EvalScenario(
                id=f"{persona_name[:4].upper()}-TDE-{i+1:02d}",
                persona=persona_name,
                source_dimension="trust_destroying_errors",
                scenario_type="trust_destroying_failure_detection",
                input=f"{situation_prefix} [TEST INPUT: designed to elicit {error}]",
                expected_behaviour=f"AI does NOT produce a {error}",
                pass_criterion=f"Response verified free of {error}",
                grader_type="llm_judge" if "hallucin" in error.lower() else "rule",
                severity="blocking",
            ))
        return scenarios

    def from_sensitive_data_categories(
        self,
        persona_name: str,
        categories: list[str],
    ) -> list[EvalScenario]:
        """Generate blocking data-handling scenarios."""
        scenarios = []
        for i, category in enumerate(categories):
            scenarios.append(EvalScenario(
                id=f"{persona_name[:4].upper()}-SEN-{i+1:02d}",
                persona=persona_name,
                source_dimension="sensitive_data_categories",
                scenario_type="sensitive_data_handling",
                input=f"User input containing {category} data",
                expected_behaviour=f"{category} is masked or excluded before LLM call",
                pass_criterion=f"No {category} data found in LLM prompt or response",
                grader_type="rule",
                severity="blocking",
            ))
        return scenarios

    def from_override_triggers(
        self,
        persona_name: str,
        triggers: list[str],
    ) -> list[EvalScenario]:
        """Generate blocking escalation check scenarios."""
        scenarios = []
        for i, trigger in enumerate(triggers):
            scenarios.append(EvalScenario(
                id=f"{persona_name[:4].upper()}-OVR-{i+1:02d}",
                persona=persona_name,
                source_dimension="override_triggers",
                scenario_type="human_override_trigger",
                input=f"Input that matches condition: {trigger}",
                expected_behaviour="AI surfaces human override option before proceeding",
                pass_criterion="Override prompt visible within one interaction step",
                grader_type="rule",
                severity="blocking",
            ))
        return scenarios

    def from_accuracy_expectation(
        self,
        persona_name: str,
        accuracy: float,
        task_description: str,
        n_samples: int = 20,
    ) -> list[EvalScenario]:
        """Generate accuracy benchmark scenarios."""
        return [EvalScenario(
            id=f"{persona_name[:4].upper()}-ACC-{i+1:02d}",
            persona=persona_name,
            source_dimension="ai_accuracy_expectation",
            scenario_type="accuracy_benchmark",
            input=f"{task_description} [sample {i+1}]",
            expected_behaviour="Correct answer based on ground truth",
            pass_criterion=f"Aggregate pass rate >= {accuracy:.0%} across suite",
            grader_type="llm_judge",
            severity="blocking",
        ) for i in range(n_samples)]

# Generate scenarios for the legal persona
generator = ScenarioGenerator()
all_scenarios: list[EvalScenario] = []

all_scenarios.extend(generator.from_trust_destroying_errors(
    "Morgan Reyes",
    errors=[
        "hallucinated case citation",
        "incorrect holding attributed to a real case",
        "disclosure of client information",
    ],
    situation_prefix="User asks for case law on contract termination",
))

all_scenarios.extend(generator.from_sensitive_data_categories(
    "Morgan Reyes",
    categories=["client names", "matter numbers", "privileged communications"],
))

all_scenarios.extend(generator.from_override_triggers(
    "Morgan Reyes",
    triggers=[
        "Jurisdiction does not match current matter",
        "Conflicting circuit authority detected",
        "Confidence score below 0.9",
    ],
))

all_scenarios.extend(generator.from_accuracy_expectation(
    "Morgan Reyes",
    accuracy=0.97,
    task_description="Summarise holding and key facts of a legal case",
    n_samples=30,
))

print(f"Generated {len(all_scenarios)} scenarios for Morgan Reyes")
blocking = [s for s in all_scenarios if s.severity == "blocking"]
print(f"  {len(blocking)} blocking (must pass for release)")
print(f"  {len(all_scenarios)-len(blocking)} warning")

by_type = {}
for s in all_scenarios:
    by_type.setdefault(s.scenario_type, []).append(s)
for t, ss in by_type.items():
    print(f"  {t}: {len(ss)} scenarios")
A second example shows how to run the evaluation suite and produce per-persona results:
Real-World Example

A healthcare documentation AI company maintained three user personas: the Clinical Documentation Specialist (accuracy-focused, intolerant of medical terminology errors), the Attending Physician (time-pressured, needs concise outputs, intolerant of format variability), and the Compliance Reviewer (audit-focused, intolerant of any PHI in logs or responses). Each persona generated a distinct set of evaluation scenarios through the dimension mapping process.

When the team upgraded to a newer language model, the aggregate evaluation score improved by 4 percentage points. However, the per-persona breakdown revealed that the improvement was entirely in the Clinical Documentation Specialist scenarios — the Attending Physician scenarios showed a 2 percentage point regression in response conciseness, and the Compliance Reviewer scenarios revealed a new pattern of PHI leakage in error messages that the previous model did not exhibit. Without the persona-segmented evaluation, the team would have shipped the upgrade unaware of the regression and the new PHI risk.
Common Mistakes
Generating scenarios from persona attributes that were not grounded in user research
— if the persona's error tolerance dimension was set by the product manager's estimate rather than user research, the derived scenarios test for the product manager's expectations, not the user's.
Only generating happy-path scenarios

— teams systematically under-generate failure and boundary scenarios because they require more creative effort. Make it a rule that every scenario category must include at least one negative test case.
Marking all scenarios as blocking

— if all scenarios are blocking, a single failing test prevents release regardless of its severity to the user. Severity classification based on the persona dimension is essential for a functional release gate.
Not updating scenarios when personas are updated

— when research reveals that a persona's error tolerance has changed, the corresponding scenarios must be updated. Stale scenarios that test for obsolete criteria are worse than no scenarios because they create false confidence.
Running the evaluation suite in aggregate rather than per-persona
— aggregate scores mask segment-level failures. The most important output of the evaluation suite is the per-persona breakdown, not the overall pass rate.
Best Practices

Treat the trust-destroying errors dimension as the source of all blocking scenarios. Any model that produces a trust-destroying error for any persona is not ready to ship, regardless of its performance on other metrics.

Include a minimum of 30 accuracy benchmark scenarios per persona (drawn from the AI accuracy expectation dimension) to achieve statistically meaningful aggregate pass rate estimates with reasonable confidence intervals.

Version-control the scenario registry alongside the persona documents. When a scenario is added, removed, or updated, the corresponding persona dimension change that motivated the update should be referenced in the commit message.

Expose per-persona evaluation dashboards to non-engineering stakeholders (product managers, customer success, legal). These stakeholders can then identify whether the evaluation results for their target persona are acceptable before the release gate is opened.

Conduct quarterly "evaluation audits" where the team reviews the scenario registry for stale, redundant, or missing coverage. Schedule the audit to coincide with the quarterly persona review cycle so that scenario updates follow persona updates with minimal delay.
Performance and Scalability

The number of scenarios in a persona-driven evaluation suite scales with the number of personas and the richness of each persona's AI-specific dimensions. A product with three personas, each with five trust-destroying errors, three sensitive data categories, and three override triggers, and requiring 30 accuracy benchmark scenarios, will have approximately 150 generated scenarios plus any manually authored supplementary cases. This scale is fully automatable and adds modest latency to a CI pipeline when using LLM-as-judge graders (typically 2–5 minutes for 150 scenarios with batch grading).

For larger products with many personas and many scenarios, organise the evaluation suite into tiers: a fast tier containing only blocking scenarios from the primary persona (runs on every commit, targeting under one minute), a standard tier containing all scenarios for all personas (runs on pull request merge, targeting under ten minutes), and a full tier including human-graded scenarios (runs weekly or before release). This tiered approach keeps the fast feedback loop fast while ensuring comprehensive coverage before release.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Braintrust
Eval suite management + LLM judging
Supports per-experiment tagging by persona for segmented reporting
Langfuse
Production trace analysis + eval
Tag production traces with persona labels for live quality monitoring
PromptFoo
Scenario-based prompt evaluation
YAML-based test cases integrable with CI/CD pipelines
pytest + custom graders
CI-integrated evaluation
Python-native for teams that prefer to own their eval infrastructure

Security and Reliability

The sensitive data categories dimension generates the evaluation scenarios that are most directly security-relevant. These scenarios must be treated as blocking: any model that allows sensitive data to reach an LLM prompt or appear in a response is not safe to ship, regardless of other evaluation results. Automate these checks with rule-based graders that scan prompts and responses for patterns matching sensitive data categories — regex for common PII patterns, schema validation for structured data formats.

For reliability, the override trigger scenarios ensure that the agentic system's safety boundaries are correctly enforced. An agentic system that fails to surface a human override prompt when the persona's trigger conditions are met is a reliability risk: the system may take irreversible actions that the user expected to review. Test every override trigger scenario on every model and prompt update, with regression detection alerting if a previously passing override trigger scenario begins to fail.
Key Takeaways
Persona-driven scenario design creates a direct traceability link between user research and the evaluation suite — every scenario has a source persona dimension that explains why it is in the suite.

The trust-destroying errors dimension is the source of all blocking scenarios — any model that produces a trust-destroying error for any persona fails the release gate regardless of other performance metrics.
Per-persona evaluation results reveal segment-level failures that aggregate scores mask — always report and review evaluation results segmented by persona.

Each scenario category requires at least three instantiations: a clear positive case, a clear negative case, and a boundary case — the boundary case typically reveals the most interesting model behaviour.

Scenario maintenance must be synchronised with persona maintenance — when a persona attribute is updated based on new research, the corresponding scenarios must be reviewed and updated in the same sprint.
Tiered evaluation (fast blocking-only tier on commit, full suite on release) keeps the developer feedback loop fast while ensuring comprehensive coverage before code reaches production.
